speaker: interviewer
text: --tape number three. We-- when we just left we were talking about pushing the German units back--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pushing, yeah, when the--

speaker: interviewer
text: --back west.

speaker: interviewee
text: After Stalingrad, there was no-- they couldn't keep it up. What-- the American was coming on one side, you know? And the Russian on the other side. They were pushing them. They were throwing everything away. They brought you on. They change a lot. They-- but there was no cooking now, for extra, for the officers.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what--

speaker: interviewer
text: How far did you go? How-- where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: How far did we go? I told you they were pushing, pushing. How far we was going? We were coming almost with Schachendorf, Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, you went through [INAUDIBLE] again. You understand? They're pushing, pushing, pushing. End of it, we-- we-- we stayed in-- in Schachendorf. Were you in Munkacs along the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was in Munkacs over there too.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. But these were all brief stops? And you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, brief stop. Two days, three days only. In Munkacs-- excuse me. In Munkacs we were then-- when they said the war is over, that the Hungarians put down the guns, they don't want to fight no more against the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, Munkacs is right on the Russian border. Did you ever see the picture of that? Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right there. Munkacs was the only city, I think, in the whole world, was 50% Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they-- they were [INAUDIBLE] Jews, you know? And from there on, they were coming there. Then I went there for that. I could go to the city and get a-- a few things, what I wanted.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were pushing us back, pushing us back with Russian again. You know? Then after Russians, they say, there's no stop. There's no reason to stay here. Again, we went back to Schachendorf.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was right on the-- that was-- a time [INAUDIBLE]. But that day we got liberated. That day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that. What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: But I can't believe it. 12 o'clock, midnight, it comes to the store. The war is over. And everything is gone. And I had two cousins there who was in the white band.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the white band mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- they were half Jews or Catholic or their mother, father. But they-- those boys said-- they said, everybody can go how they want to. But I says-- I went over to there.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, Stevie, George, come on. In a half hour, you free. We are-- we were hearding from-- from Schachendorf how long they were-- that was right next to [INAUDIBLE], where we went out first, by the border. We were holding all the guns and everything. I says, commander, what are you going for, there? Oh, they not going to hurt us no more. So what did they do? There was about 120 of these white band. Those son-of-a-bitches Hungarians-- Hungarian Germans, they were.

speaker: interviewee
text: They put them in a barn. Now, this is the truth. This is true worst thing I ever heard in my life. They put them in a barn. They threw around, gasoline. And while they were running around, they shot every 120 of them. This is the truth.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is after liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: After liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these were cousins of yours?

speaker: interviewee
text: My cousins' two kids. [INAUDIBLE] boys. Where I told you, beautiful, big, tall guy-- they said, [INAUDIBLE], they're not going to hurt us. We're the [INAUDIBLE]. We always-- we're-- we got a white band. They were maybe eight years old when they convert. That was the worst. Then we work--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that-- how-- I mean, how did-- how did that make you feel in terms of-- how did you feel towards the Hungarians at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lost everything. I-- I said, there is no God. How did-- how could they kill those little kids there, 2 to 10 years old? How could they kill them?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do? Did they hurt you? you? you? They didn't hurt nobody. They're born in the right-- wrong place at the wrong time. When I come back, I went right away to-- after the war, I went there, where they lived. And they asked me, did-- did-- did I see the boys? [INAUDIBLE] was one of them. They were brought up Catholic, these two boys. You know? And I told them what happened. They got a big memorial put up for the two boys, for [INAUDIBLE], when I go to the Seder during my aunt's, they buried. She was 83 years old. They convert.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's about 12 miles from [INAUDIBLE], where we used to live in-- in the-- in the winter. I showed you. And they-- my aunts and my-- they have here, [INAUDIBLE] And they take-- those-- those old people take care of theirs, where they buried. And for the two boys, they put up a big monument in [INAUDIBLE]. They loved those two boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. How did it feel to you, that it was not just the Germans? That it was also Hungarians? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Some Hungarians was worse what the Germans. I told you, when you want for water-- we want a drink of water. Should I tell you about my cousin? My cousin that-- that somebody tell your husband what he done with his wife?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I don't know you girls know it. But in Europe, that time was that the younger cannot get-- the younger cannot get married so long the older is not married. My aunt had three daughters, Olga, [INAUDIBLE] and [? Terry. ?] Terry was the youngest. Olga was the oldest. And [INAUDIBLE] was the middle one. In 1919, my father brought up Olga for two weeks to see Chicago. [INAUDIBLE] fall in love there with a schoolteacher. His was [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and but she swore for her mother, how long she's alive, she's never going to marry Steve. For he's a [INAUDIBLE]. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: 20 Years, she was her girlfriend. She lived there in the hotel. Every morning they brought him up the breakfast. Then he went to school. Night, his supper. He was the big boss. They had a three-story building, a hotel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the Germans come in [INAUDIBLE]-- I showed you where is that, right on the-- on the Yugoslav [INAUDIBLE]-- on the Yugoslav border. You know? Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: The wealthiest people. There were three girls and one boy. All right, the-- the-- the two younger-- the younger one and the older they took away. And the brother, Joe, they took him away. And the parents died but 10 years before that. So he tells his wife, [INAUDIBLE], don't worry. I'll hide you. You won't have no problem. He hide her for awhile, for two, three weeks. That minute when the Germans comes in, that son-of-a-- I don't want to call him-- was the first to tell where his wife is hiding. He never thought the Jewish people are going to come back from this concentration camp. There was two boys with us whose name were [INAUDIBLE], from the capital, [INAUDIBLE]. One of them, [INAUDIBLE], was the European champion in that. So he used to bring up, in the black market, veal and this, and what you couldn't sell at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: He says, you know what your brother-in-law done? I says, no. He said, he was the first one to tell where his wife was hiding. We took care of him. What do you mean, you took care of him?

speaker: interviewee
text: We took care of that son-of-a-bitch.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean? Tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: We killed him. We kicked him to death.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who? Who did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four of us, five of us. He didn't deserve more.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is after liberation, in Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know I was wrong. How would you like-- you see, what I told you I was in a very exclusive store in Budapest, every morning we had-- we had to put-- you know, those heavy-- you know, the white thing on. Moniker-- moniker every day. I went to a barber there for 8 or 10 years. He had there a barber. He he was a hunchback.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please sit.

speaker: interviewee
text: And every-- every Sunday, he come up to shave me. Well, I give them a good tip. And that minute, when Hitler comes in, I go in to take a shave. You goddamn I-don't-know-what-kind-of-Jew. Get outta here or I'll cut your neck.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you feel? The guy was going, thank you, thank you. I-- it's all right. Eight years, he always got a good tip.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eight years. Every Sunday morning, come to shave me. With a good tip, everything. At that minute, Hitler comes and he was the-- he was the first to say, get out of this chair. I'll cut your neck.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there a lot that you know of? Was there a lot of retaliation? Was there a lot--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a lot of retaliation, in every city in the world. Look what was in Italy, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I want to know about your home. I want to know what you saw.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Budapest, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what you know of.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I didn't see this. But I come back later on. You understand?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- I come much later. And Budapest was liberated much before. There were-- wait a minute. You must know where it was. There-- how you come out from the-- the [INAUDIBLE], where that girl was there. There was-- every day they put up there-- they-- they hanged up a couple of these Nazis there. I never saw that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you, I never saw these things. What, I never did. When I come back to Budapest, it was normal life then, by that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. On your way back, did you see any other examples of revenge or retaliation?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. We didn't want no revenge. We just wanted to come home.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that day I was liberated, today is 52 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is the anniversary?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I never thought it. Especially when I was in Lemberg, in a death role.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find, when you went back to Budapest, any members of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody in the world. Except my niece, in Israel. Not my niece, my cousin's daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Friends, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were friends back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they were friends who they-- who they-- they were in the Swedish home. Just only my aunt.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. But nobody else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody got killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find out what had happened to them? Or where people had disappeared?

speaker: interviewee
text: I put ad in all kinds of paper. I looking for persons. When I was in Washington DC, with the Holocaust, when Reagan was there, all kinds of ads, asking for Reichfeld or [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't find nobody, anyone. But the mistake, when I was in Budapest, I told my cousin's daughters in Israel, I didn't even know her name. I told you that. I didn't even know her name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you tell me about this? How did you find somebody?

speaker: interviewee
text: When-- when I was there they said that [INAUDIBLE], well, I saw your sister, brother, right away in the gas, what were too little. She's in Israel. I says, where? She says, we don't know what's the name. They said, we don't know where they took off for different kinds of name.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, who knows that? [INAUDIBLE], when I got back from Budapest, I called her right away up. She says, somebody in Haifa knows that-- where she is and what's her name. I says, give me the lady's number. I know the name is-- lady's number was Smith. Her husband was a lawyer at that time. So she says to me, call me in two weeks. I called her in two weeks. She says, [? George, ?] I still don't know where she lives. I says, give me this lady's name. Excuse me.

speaker: interviewee
text: She give me his-- this-- this lawyer's name. I call her up. I says, I heard you know where [INAUDIBLE] lives in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: She says, who are you? I say, she's my cousin's daughter. I'm [? George Reichfeld. ?] I don't know you, I says. I know that I says, I don't think you know me. I says, could you tell me what's her name and where she lives? She says to me, if you call me back a week, I'll let you know. I'll-- I know-- I don't-- she lives in the kibbutz, I showed you, right on the Lebanon border, where the Jordan River is. You know? Well, I got pictures from there. And I called her a week. She says look, I still don't know. I says, you know what? Next week I'm going to be in-- in Tel Aviv.

speaker: interviewee
text: I told my girlfriend, come on, Jean, let's go to Israel. So we went to Tel Aviv. I called this lady up. She started to talk to me, Hebrew. I don't even understand Jewish, not Hebrew. So I went down to the desk. There was a lot of Romanian there-- Romanian Jews. They all took Hebrew. And-- and she write up 10 Hebrew name in the kibbutz. The first seven, I only know two names-- Susan, a Hungarian. Find Susan, a Hungarian in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: The first seven says they never heard, they never heard, they never heard. Then I come out. Right as you come out from the Hilton, there's a little police station. I says, you speak English?

speaker: interviewee
text: [EXHALES]

speaker: interviewer
text: He says, a little. I says, will you please call this dame up? Susan, a Hungarian. But-- this kibbutz, there was just three kibbutz left over.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, no. There was two left over. No. He calls the third one. He says, she don't live no more in the kibbutz. She lives down in the village. I don't want to-- [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: I says, will you call her, please? She called her up. He called her up. I said, [INAUDIBLE]. She was surprised. Nobody know her [INAUDIBLE]. I said, [INAUDIBLE]. They [INAUDIBLE]. But you are you. [INAUDIBLE], I says, this is [INAUDIBLE]. She must fall down and fainted. Two minutes later, her husband picks her up the phone. Who are you? Who are you? And then [INAUDIBLE] come to the phone. I don't want to tell you how we feeled. She says, right away, the Lebanon the border, I showed you where, where the Jordan River is, I says, [INAUDIBLE], I'm coming out tomorrow. She says, [? George, ?] you'll never find that. So next day morning, she says, George-- I says, when you coming? She says, we get up a little later. But I'll be there, 11 o'clock.

speaker: interviewee
text: I told my girlfriend, here's some money. Go out shopping. When-- when she comed in there, in the Hilton, you know, I don't want to tell you what a relief was that. Only one left over.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you find her? [EXHALES]

speaker: interviewee
text: I got there on the cape-- whenever I did-- 10 years, 11 years ago now. 12 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: So late '80s.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was twice in-- I-- I bring them up to Boston. They were twice here. And now she-- she goes to Budapest to fix up her teeth as one for [INAUDIBLE]. So I-- I-- I take a place for them to the-- in the-- in the [INAUDIBLE], and the market island. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the life story. Nobody left out--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you found her about 10 years ago, after all these years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, 12-- 12 years exactly now.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I want to go back to Budapest following the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you make a living after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war, I-- well, should I be honest? I'm honest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Absolutely, be honest.

speaker: interviewee
text: First, honest, I got first a job. The farmers used to bring in gooses, chicken eggs. And I used to help them take it off from the truck. I got a pretty nice pay.

speaker: interviewer
text: One day, a lady brings-- at that time you couldn't bring in bacon or this or that. And she says, I got here 10 pounds of bacon. 10 kilo bacon. Can you sell it? And then the little store start open. There was a butcher named Schwartz. And I know the guy a long time ago. I go to him. I says, [INAUDIBLE], can you use 10 kilo bacon? So yeah, he says, sure. How much you give it to-- how much you pay me? He said, how much you got? I says, about 10 kilo. He said, I'll give you 80 cents for a kilo.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went back. I said, I just got 60 cents. That's good. So I made $2 on it. She said, but if you want I can bring you a whole hog in. A whole hog in. Brought in the hog. I made 50 bucks on it. Then I heard that they bring bacon and eggs. You get-- you can get a permit to bring for the English, American, French, and the Russian embassy, all these chickens, eggs, no bacon and no fat. No nothing of these kinds of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I got in touch there with a Russian, who know how to do it. So what did we do? We went out in the farm. In the farm we bought salt. And we went to Romania to buy salt for two cents a kilo that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Went to the farmers. We give them four-- four kilo salt for a kilo bacon. And for a kilo bacon, we got a gram gold.

speaker: interviewer
text: So our money got 40 times. We doubled our money in a day. In two days. So end of it, business was so-- but easy.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] told me, forget it. I was the biggest. I had over four trucks going every two weeks up to Vienna. The bottom was the bacon, the lard, the butter. On the top was eggs, chicken, cheese, turkey. And I had a thing. They paid everything in dollar. So I had the right to have dollars on me. But I never had a-- you know how it is. That's-- that what was to be my last trip.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that legal?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was not legal. You asked me--

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was black market?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was black market. You ask me a question. I'll tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Was that relatively common at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody done it. Everybody done it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was it just you and a couple of friends? Or was it more organized?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there was organized-- there was every-- don't forget in Vienna, at that time, you couldn't get nothing. And in there, in all of those countries, in Vienna, at that time, was over 100 Hungarian Christians who was with coal and diamonds and everything. They the one who made the whole business. End of it, I didn't want to do it. [INAUDIBLE]-- [INAUDIBLE] says, [? George, ?] you got enough. You got enough. But you know, the money, it was coming in. If we wanted to buy a bag of sugar, you needed a bag-- there-- there, you know, there was no money. 100 blue money, 50 gray money, this in color money. Even-- you didn't even know what you payed for something. The money was coming in. You wouldn't believe it. People got millionaire. That's the reason you see so many big Hungarian guys is all full of money. They're multimillionaire. They didn't have time to stop the black market. And I was cut. At that time, the captain was named Dr. [INAUDIBLE]. He says to me, [? George, ?] get out of this business. I had, at that time, 100 Napoleon. That's a gold 20 franc. It's a rooster on it. They took everything away. And I-- I run away with a suit and a toothbrush. I never looked back.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: When was that? In 19-- I tell you I had to pay for-- that must be 1946, '47.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did--

speaker: interviewee
text: '47. I went to Vienna first, to the Rothschild's. Then we went in Germany, different camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: So displaced persons camp? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. Yeah. I was in Ulm, where Einstein was born. I was there. There it come out aloud that those people who was born in Chicago-- was born in United States can go, right away, back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went up to Munich. And I says, I was born in Chicago, this and this. My father died there.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were American citizen in 1921. I used to live on Cedric Street, North Hudson Street. I went to school. Everything was shine. Two weeks later, they called me in. Get packed and come into Bremerhaven. But I didn't tell them I give up my citizenship. I went back. Just before, two hours, the ship would start from Bremerhaven, they took me off the ship. He says, you're a trader. You give up your citizenship. Back. And I stayed [INAUDIBLE]. And then I come back in 1950-- '52.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for those intervening four years, were you staying in Germany? Or were you back in Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was staying in Germany. I was in Ulm, [INAUDIBLE]. I was-- I was working there too. I was playing soccer there. And I was working-- working in the warehouse. I had two jobs there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the end of it, in 1952, they-- all of camps were [INAUDIBLE] then. I come back to United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: What--

speaker: interviewee
text: My first job was here, I was a chicken flicker. And a broad [INAUDIBLE] in a street corner.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- how-- what were your feelings about coming back to the United States at that point? How did that feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I want to tell you something you don't believe. My friend Larry, there, I would-- it's funny thing. We-- the same boat we went back to Hungary, that time, the George Washington, we were coming back with the same boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: But 50 people there was waiting for me in the-- on the-- in the what's the name? On the piers, in-- you know, in Long Island, where the boat comes in. And my friends, they're waiting for me there. I says, how did you know? He says, I read it in the paper that George Reichfeld was born in Chicago and went back to Hungary and went through everything, is coming back to the United States. And he was waiting for me. And how did you know him? From where?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Germany. We lived together. Actually, he's a wonderful guy. I'll show you a picture of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for work when you got back here?

speaker: interviewee
text: First I was a chicken flicker.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And then after?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a cake-- I learned to be a cake baker in Brookline, by Feldman's Bakery, next to the KI.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Do you have family? Did you marry? Do you have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bad marriage. I told you-- I told you again, I was married. I was married. Just with-- with [? Dustin ?] [? Hoffman, ?] she left me there with two kids. I never saw her. Now it's over 40 years. I didn't even see her. I don't know where the hell she is.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I asked the kids, who wants to-- if you get married, you want to see the kids-- your mother? They said, what kind of mother? Did she ever say-- see us? I don't even know where she is. I heard that she had three more kids. That's my life story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your sons. What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: One is-- one is Steven. One is Benji.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old-- when were they born?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- they were-- they're now 40 years old. And 42. One-- she-- she had Benji before I married her. She had a-- a-- the father was an Irish guy. Her-- my ex-wife's father was a Greek Jew. The mother was a Christian.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your-- your ex-wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you characterize your life since coming back to this country, compared to life in Hungary? [EXHALES]

speaker: interviewee
text: Should I be honest with you?

speaker: interviewer
text: Absolutely.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some way, God was good to me. And they always say, a working man will make a living, never going to be rich. Right? I had some money coming back from Hungary. And my friend, Larry, there, we first started a little smutty business, making woman panties. And then from panties we went to sportswear, this and that. I had a marvelous life in United States. It could not be better. I traveled three times, all over the world. Yesterday night they were talking about Bali. And I said, what a wonderful time I had there in Bali too. And I just looked at-- there in-- then when I went twice to Alaska, I loved it. I went to the World's Fair. I come to this country. I couldn't-- nothing. And my first date, I had a date by [INAUDIBLE]. I don't think-- at that time [INAUDIBLE] was, on Beacon Street, a very good restaurant. And then my first date-- my first-- on Saturday, I was the happiest guy, when I first bought a-- a blue suit by Ripley's. I was looking that everybody's looking at me, that I got a suit. I have no complaints. And never thought I'd get sick. 42 years ago I had a heart attack. 40 years, I didn't even go to a doctor. I never took a pill in. I traveled a lot. The whole summer, every year, I stayed on the Cape in Centerville, a two-bedroom apartment. In January, I always go to Acapulco for two weeks. In May, to Budapest, Hungary. Summer on the Cape. And fall, I go away again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I can say, right, God was good to me. I have no complaints. But now, listen here, all of-- see, what I told you, 40 years later, 42, for 42 years ago I had a heart attack. 40 years, I didn't even go to a doctor. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got angina. I got a pacemaker. I got a dislocated disc. And I take 14 pills in a day. And I still date. I still go out. I'm not going to feel sorry for myself. I-- if I would feel sorry for myself, I don't think I could live that long. And I better have 100 friends to 1 enemy. I used to live in Norwood, in the [? Windsor ?] [? Garden. ?] Nobody-- nobody know my name, except the Cookie Man. Where every day I brought the kids a big box of chocolate chip cookies or cupcakes, or half and half. I used to take them to the children's zoo. In the winter I-- I rented out the snowmobiles. I used to take the kids around. I had a pretty-- I tell you why, because I told you. I've never smoked. I never drinked. The only thing, I gambled when I got married. No, I used to gamble. I used to go up to Foxborough, with my date, give her $20 to play. But I'm not a gambler. I love the theater. And I love traveling. And I like nice restaurants.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And nice ladies.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any message that you would like to leave us with about your experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Number one, be good to yourself. How long you don't hurt nobody, it's nobody's goddamn business what you do. And the most important thing in your life, try to meet the right people. Try always to look a little higher, where you are. I can show you my last week lady friends was all doctors and this. And I couldn't-- my first one was in Worcester, the wealthiest woman there. She says, George, you're not educated. But you're enough intelligence. And what I told you, try to-- try to look a little higher where you are. Well, I told you I got no schooling. I told you that. And I'm very careful who I go out with, where I live. I love nice restaurants. And I love the theater. And I love-- traveling has gone by me. I don't think I can travel again. You imagine, go down to Florida, go down to Budapest, go down to Israel in nine months.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK. Thank you for speaking with us today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what I told you-- number one, be good to yourself. That's the most important thing in the world. You think I'm right or wrong?

speaker: interviewer
text: Absolutely, I agree.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you're no good to yourself, you're no good to nobody. That's the way I feel. Maybe I'm wrong.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're very welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: On on the left-hand side is my father. His name was-- [INAUDIBLE] or Riechfeld. Right next to him there is a-- name is [INAUDIBLE]. He's a Hungarian. He was a good friend of my father. Next is [INAUDIBLE]. He's a friend of my father. And this guy who's coming up, his name is [INAUDIBLE], Frank [INAUDIBLE]. His-- his son was my best friend. After that, I don't know what happened. This-- this guy's a Polish guy, I don't know. And this name is [INAUDIBLE]. He's a German.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Chicago, 1920, on Sedgwick Street, by Oscar Mayer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Remember the factory down there on Sedgwick Street? No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nm-mm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Way down.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm. I was young. [LAUGHS] Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: The left-hand side is Benji. He was, there, about a year and four months old, I think. Today he's 42.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: With him is Steven. He's a year and two months younger what he is.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, do you know when this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in 1955.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Boston?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Boston, Brookline. On Harvard Street, 406 Harvard--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: 406 Harvard Street.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my cousin, only live relative I got left over. The whole family got killed. Her name is Susan. What's her Jewish name? I don't even know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's her last name today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't even know her last Jewish name.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is [INAUDIBLE]. It was originally [INAUDIBLE], but it come from Hungary, from [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken 10 years ago, exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Israel, right on Lebanon-- oh, right on the Lebanon border, in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thanks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son, Steven. This was taken on the Lebanon Jordan River, but 10 years ago too. And that's me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's a doctor. He's 6' 3'. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
